summary:
The invention of writing in Sumer between approximately 3400 and 3100 BCE represents one of the defining moments in human intellectual history, transforming the capacity of societies to store knowledge, coordinate activity, and transmit culture across generations. The earliest Sumerian writing emerged from the administrative needs of the great temple institutions of the [[event:1617|Uruk Period]], where tracking agricultural surpluses, labor obligations, and trade goods demanded record-keeping beyond the capacity of human memory.

The earliest Sumerian written documents are clay tablets impressed with a stylus, recording commodity allocations and accounting tallies in a pictographic script. These tablets were not literature or religious texts but bureaucratic tools — receipts, ration lists, and inventories. The temples of Uruk, which functioned as redistributive economic centers managing enormous quantities of grain, animals, and manufactured goods, generated hundreds of such tablets, which have been excavated at sites including Uruk and Jemdet Nasr.

Over the course of the fourth millennium BCE, this proto-cuneiform system evolved rapidly. The signs were simplified and abstracted, and phonetic values were assigned to symbols that had previously been purely logographic. This phonetic extension allowed the writing system to represent proper names, prepositions, and grammatical elements that had no simple pictographic equivalent, transforming writing from an accounting tool into a medium capable of representing language itself. The resulting fully developed [[event:1619|cuneiform script]] would eventually encode the complete literary and intellectual production of Sumerian and later Akkadian civilization.

The Sumerian invention of writing had consequences that extended far beyond Mesopotamia. Knowledge of writing spread eastward to Elam (which developed the related [[event:1645|Proto-Elamite script]]) and influenced writing traditions across the ancient Near East, ultimately contributing — directly or indirectly — to the development of alphabetic scripts that are the ancestors of most modern writing systems.
